content.problemStatement:
You have an array AAA of NNN integers. 

A pair of indices (i,j)(i, j)(i,j) is called Beautiful if Ai=Aj2A_i = A_j^2Ai​=Aj2​ and 1≤i<j≤N1 \leq i \lt j \leq N1≤i<j≤N.

Count the number of Beautiful Pairs in the given array.

TaskUse the Tree Map to solve this task.


Input Format

The first line of the input contains a single integer NNN, denoting the length of array AAA.
The second line of the input contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ — denoting the array AAA.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: first line contains an integer N, the length of the array. Second line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the number of beautiful pairs, i.e. the number of index pairs (i, j) with 1 ≤ i < j ≤ N and Ai = (Aj)².

sampleTestCases.explanation: Only pair (2,3) satisfies the condition as 4 = 2².

solution.approach: Scan the array from left to right while maintaining a map that stores how many times each value has appeared so far. For the current element x (treated as Aj), if x <= 31622 (⌊√10^9⌋) we compute sq = x * x. All previously seen elements equal to sq form beautiful pairs with the current index, so we add the current frequency of sq to the answer. Afterwards we increase the frequency of x in the map. This works because the condition Ai = (Aj)² only involves the earlier element being the square of the later one, and the square of any Aj larger than 31622 exceeds the maximum possible Ai (10^9). The map can be a balanced binary search tree (TreeMap) or an unordered hash map; both give O(log N) or O(1) average update/query time.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case when using a TreeMap (ordered map). Using a hash map gives O(N) expected time.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) – the map stores at most N distinct values.
